threats.title: PEMCertChainLib notBefore Date Tag Validation Logic Bug Accepts Invalid Certificate Tags
threats.hypothesis_description: The notBefore date tag validation in PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert contains a logic error. The condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` simplifies to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)`. This means: (1) Valid GeneralizedTime (0x18) certificates are REJECTED, causing DoS for legitimate attestations. (2) Invalid/arbitrary tags (e.g., 0x05, 0x30) for notBefore are ACCEPTED, allowing certificates with garbage notBefore timestamps to pass validation. X509DateUtils.toTimestamp would parse arbitrary bytes, potentially producing notBefore=0 which makes `block.timestamp > certs[i].notBefore` always true.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect in a critical path. The intended check should be `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag != 0x18)` to reject non-standard tags. The current code inverts the logic for the notBefore tag while correctly handling notAfter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` is logically equivalent to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)`, rejecting valid GeneralizedTime while accepting arbitrary invalid tags

preconditions: A certificate is submitted for verification that uses GeneralizedTime (0x18) for notBefore, OR
A crafted certificate with an invalid notBefore tag type is submitted
The certificate is part of an SGX attestation quote verification chain

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Boolean logic error in tag validation condition
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18` is equivalent to `notBeforeTag == 0x18`, not the intended `notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag != 0x18`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Correct condition should reject any tag that is neither 0x17 nor 0x18

analysis_questions: Do any Intel PCK certificates use GeneralizedTime for notBefore dates?
What timestamp does X509DateUtils.toTimestamp produce when given bytes with invalid tag-encoded data?
Can the garbage timestamp cause notBefore=0, making expiry check always pass?
validation_requirements: Craft a certificate with invalid notBefore tag and trace through decodeCert
Test with GeneralizedTime (0x18) notBefore tag to confirm rejection
Verify the impact on _verifyCertChain with manipulated timestamps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker provides an SGX quote with a crafted PCK certificate containing a non-standard notBefore tag (e.g., 0x05). The tag passes the broken validation. X509DateUtils.toTimestamp parses garbage bytes, producing a very early notBefore timestamp (possibly 0), making the certificate appear valid. Combined with a valid signature chain, the attestation could pass with a cert that should have been rejected.
prerequisites: Access to verifyAttestation or verifyParsedQuote entry point
Ability to craft a certificate chain with valid signatures

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Compromise of SGX attestation verification integrity, potentially allowing invalid SGX proofs to be accepted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on SGX attestation verification
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract redeployment or attestation contract address update

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for GeneralizedTime notBefore tags and invalid tag types

threats.title: Certificate Chain Verification Skips Root CA Expiry and Self-Signature Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _verifyCertChain, for a 3-cert chain [PCK, Intermediate, Root], the loop breaks at i=1 when the issuer (root) pubkey hash matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH. This means the root certificate (certs[2]) is NEVER validated for expiry (notBefore/notAfter) or self-signature. An expired root CA certificate would be accepted as valid.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop's early termination at the root pubkey hash match means the root cert's notBefore/notAfter timestamps are never checked. While the trust is anchored to the public key hash, proper X.509 chain validation requires all certificates including the root to be within their validity period.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop breaks when issuerPubKeyHash == ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH at i=1, never reaching i=2 to validate root cert expiry

preconditions: Attacker provides a certificate chain with a root CA cert that has correct pubkey hash but expired validity dates
Intermediate and PCK certs are properly signed and valid

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: certChainCanBeTrusted = true; break; exits before root cert i=n-1 validation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation of root cert after pubkey hash match

analysis_questions: Can an expired Intel root CA certificate still produce valid attestation chains?
What is the actual validity period of the Intel SGX Root CA certificate?
Does the root cert self-signature skip create any signature forgery opportunity?
validation_requirements: Trace _verifyCertChain with a 3-cert chain and confirm root cert check is skipped
Test with an expired root CA certificate and valid intermediate/PCK certs
Verify that the loop correctly processes all cert indices

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker provides a certificate chain where the Intel SGX Root CA certificate has expired. The loop breaks at i=1 after matching ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH, never checking certs[2].notAfter < block.timestamp. The attestation passes with an expired trust anchor.
prerequisites: Possession of a cert chain with expired root CA but valid intermediate certs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attestation system accepts quotes anchored to expired trust roots
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on attestation integrity

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for expired root CA certificates in chain validation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Not detectable without root cert expiry monitoring

threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Has No Ownership Transfer Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation uses a custom owner variable set in the constructor with no transfer or renounce mechanism. Unlike EssentialContract-derived contracts that use Ownable2Step, this contract provides no way to change the owner. If the owner key is lost, all admin functions become permanently inaccessible. If compromised, the attacker has irrevocable control.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract manages critical attestation configuration (TCB info, QE identity, revocation lists, trusted enclaves). The inability to rotate ownership means a single key compromise permanently compromises all attestation security, and key loss permanently freezes configuration updates.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: AutomataDcapV3Attestation is deployed with `new AutomataDcapV3Attestation(address(sigVerifyLib), address(pemCertChainLib))` — deployer becomes permanent owner with no transfer mechanism

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = msg.sender in constructor; no transferOwnership function exists

preconditions: Owner key is lost or compromised
Intel updates SGX infrastructure requiring config changes

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Custom owner pattern with no transfer mechanism
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No transferOwnership, no renounceOwnership, no 2-step transfer
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use Ownable or Ownable2Step from OpenZeppelin

analysis_questions: Is the deployer address a multisig or an EOA?
Can the attestation contract be replaced via address manager registration?
What is the fallback plan if owner key is compromised?
validation_requirements: Confirm no ownership transfer function exists in the contract
Verify the deployment script sets deployer as permanent owner
Check if address manager can swap the attestation contract address

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the owner's private key is compromised, the attacker irrevocably controls attestation configuration: they can whitelist arbitrary MRENCLAVE/MRSIGNER values, remove all certificate revocations, and reconfigure TCB info to accept compromised enclaves. There is no way to transfer ownership to revoke their access.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete compromise of attestation trust model if key is compromised; frozen config if key is lost
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on SGX attestation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would require deploying a new contract and updating address manager registration

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for ownership transfer scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Owner key compromise may be undetectable until malicious config changes

threats.title: PEMCertChainLib _findTcb Array Out-of-Bounds When PCESVN Is Not Last SVN Entry
threats.hypothesis_description: In _findTcb, cpusvns array has size SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE (16) but the loop iterates SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1 (17) times. When the PCESVN_OID is encountered, the value is stored in pcesvn (not cpusvns). If PCESVN_OID is NOT the last entry in the DER sequence, the 17th iteration (i=16) would attempt cpusvns[16], causing an out-of-bounds revert in Solidity 0.8.x. Additionally, if PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns has a zero-valued gap at that index.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code assumes PCESVN_OID is always the last SVN component. If Intel changes the PCK certificate format or the SVN ordering changes, all attestation verification would permanently revert. The zero-gap issue could also cause incorrect TCB level matching.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cpusvns = new uint256[](16); loop 0..16 (17 iterations); cpusvns[i] written when i=16 and not PCESVN causes OOB

preconditions: PCK certificate has PCESVN_OID not as the last SVN entry in the DER sequence
Or an extra non-PCESVN SVN component exists at position 16

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Array sized 16 with loop iterating 17 times; out-of-bounds write if PCESVN not last
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cpusvns[i] = cpusvn where i can be 16 (array size 16, valid indices 0-15)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check on i before array write; no explicit handling of PCESVN position

analysis_questions: What is the actual ordering of SVN components in Intel PCK certificates?
Is PCESVN always the last component in current Intel SGX certificate implementations?
Does the zero-gap in cpusvns affect TCB level comparison results?
validation_requirements: Parse a real Intel PCK certificate and verify SVN ordering
Test with PCESVN_OID at non-final position to trigger OOB
Verify cpusvns values with PCESVN at different positions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Intel changes PCK certificate format such that PCESVN is not the last SVN entry, _findTcb would attempt cpusvns[16] (OOB), causing all verifyAttestation calls to revert. This would constitute a permanent DoS of the attestation system.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of attestation verification if triggered
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on SGX attestation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract redeployment with fixed parsing

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-standard SVN ordering in PCK certificates

threats.title: TaikoL2 gasExcess=0 Permanently Disables Dynamic Base Fee
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calc1559BaseFee, when gasExcess is 0, the entire base fee calculation is skipped and basefee returns 1 wei. The function only adds _parentGasUsed to gasExcess if gasExcess > 0 initially. This means once gasExcess reaches 0, it stays at 0 permanently, making the base fee permanently stuck at 1 wei regardless of network congestion.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code path `if (gasExcess > 0)` gates all gas excess accumulation. If gasExcess somehow reaches 0 (through large issuance subtraction exceeding accumulated excess), the dynamic fee mechanism permanently breaks. The comment says gasExcess=0 means 'dynamic 1559 base fee is disabled' but there's no recovery mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (gasExcess > 0) gates all basefee computation; gasExcess=0 is an absorbing state

preconditions: gasExcess reaches 0 through issuance exceeding accumulated excess
OR gasExcess is initialized to 0
Note: excess is clamped to min 1, but if gasExcess starts at 0 it stays at 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: gasExcess=0 is absorbing state with no recovery
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: excess = excess > issuance ? excess - issuance : 1 prevents reaching 0 from non-zero, but initial 0 persists
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to re-enable dynamic fee if gasExcess becomes 0

analysis_questions: Can gasExcess be initialized to 0 through TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable.setConfigAndExcess?
What happens if issuance calculation causes gasExcess to wrap to 0?
Is there a recovery path if dynamic fee is accidentally disabled?
validation_requirements: Verify gasExcess=0 makes basefee permanently return 1
Test whether setConfigAndExcess can set gasExcess back to non-zero
Trace full basefee calculation with gasExcess=0 initial state

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If gasExcess is set to 0 (via TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable.setConfigAndExcess or initial deployment), the L2 base fee becomes permanently 1 wei. This enables near-free spam transactions on the L2 network, potentially degrading network performance.
prerequisites: gasExcess reaches 0 through configuration or calculation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Near-free L2 transactions enable spam attacks degrading network quality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users affected by network congestion from spam
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can call setConfigAndExcess to restore non-zero gasExcess

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for gasExcess=0 persistence and recovery

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None for spam; requires owner access for initial trigger

threats.title: Unbounded on-chain workload in DCAP quote verification enables practical gas-DoS of any protocol path that requires attestation verification
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation performs multiple heavy parsing + cryptographic verification steps over user-supplied quotes (ASN.1 traversal, Base64 decode, multiple signature verifications). If any production pathway (e.g., SGX-based proving/registration) calls verifyAttestation/verifyParsedQuote inside a state-changing transaction, an attacker can submit adversarial quotes that are expensive to process but ultimately fail late, causing consistent out-of-gas / revert and blocking the operation (or making it economically infeasible).
threats.investigation_rationale: The code does not implement explicit gas bounding, early cheap reject ordering beyond a few checks, or rate-limiting. Heavy operations include DER parsing and multiple signature checks. A single expensive failing path can be used to grief contract functions that must verify attestations to proceed.
deployment_assumptions: Some state-changing protocol function calls AutomataDcapV3Attestation.verifyAttestation(...) or verifyParsedQuote(...) and reverts if verification fails
That function is permissionless or attacker-reachable (e.g., instance registration, proof submission, etc.)
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys and registers AutomataDcapV3Attestation as 'automata_dcap_attestation', implying it is a live dependency; however, the calling contracts (e.g., SgxVerifier) are out of snippet scope here.

proof_of_signal.pattern: user-controlled quote triggers Base64 decode + ASN.1 DER parse + multiple sig verifications; no gas bounds; failures can happen late after expensive work

preconditions: Attacker can call (directly or indirectly) a state-changing function that relies on verifyAttestation/verifyParsedQuote
That state-changing function does not cap gas or pre-validate offchain
Target chain has typical block gas limits where these operations can approach limits

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: expensive verification on untrusted input without explicit gas bounds
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: late-failing quotes can maximize consumed gas then revert, enabling repeated griefing
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no explicit max sizes for nested elements beyond minimal checks; no precompile failure handling beyond boolean returns

analysis_questions: Which production state-changing functions call verifyAttestation/verifyParsedQuote?
What are the maximum observed gas costs for worst-case failing quotes (late failure after cert parsing/signature checks)?
Can an attacker craft inputs that pass early checks but fail only after the most expensive steps?
validation_requirements: Gas profiling fuzzing over quote bytes/parsed structs to maximize gas before failure
Trace integration call graph from SGX verifier / instance registry into attestation contract
Measure whether repeated calls can reliably hit block gas limit or make tx economically non-viable

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls an SGX-gated entry point with a crafted quote that passes parse + cert decode, then fails at the last signature check, consuming near-max gas and reverting; legitimate users cannot complete the operation within acceptable gas.
prerequisites: Attacker funds for repeated gas griefing
A protocol path that performs attestation verification on-chain

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but material: prevents claiming, proving, registering, or other SGX-dependent operations; may stall core protocol components relying on SGX verification
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on SGX/attestation-gated operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Likely requires contract upgrade or moving expensive steps offchain

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing adversarial gas-maximization tests for quote verification

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: profile worst-case quotes; identify dominant cost centers (Base64, ASN.1, signature verifies)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: on-chain reverts and high gas usage observable

threats.title: ERC20Airdrop2.claim() allows repeated claims for same user across multiple leaves (different amounts) due to leaf hash design mismatch with per-user accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Airdrop2.claim stores claimedAmount[user] += amount, but replay protection is keyed by hash = keccak256(abi.encode("CLAIM_TAIKO_AIRDROP", abi.encode(user, amount))). If the Merkle tree includes multiple leaves for the same user with different amounts (intentionally or by mistake), the same user can successfully claim multiple times (each leaf hash is distinct) and accumulate amounts, potentially exceeding intended allocation.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level mismatch: replay protection is per-leaf, but accounting is per-user additive. Many airdrops intend one allocation per user. If tree construction ever produces multiple leaves per user (common in sharded lists, migration, or error), this contract will honor all of them.
deployment_assumptions: The configured merkleRoot contains (or could be updated to contain) more than one leaf for the same user with different amounts
Project intent is one-time allocation per user, not multiple additive allocations
threats.config_evidence: MerkleClaimable.setConfig allows owner to set arbitrary merkleRoot; no on-chain enforcement of unique user leaf constraint.

inventory_refs.function: claim(address user, uint256 amount, bytes32[] calldata proof)

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimedAmount[user] += amount while MerkleClaimable._verifyClaim uniqueness is per (user,amount) leaf hash, not per user

preconditions: A merkleRoot is published that contains multiple valid leaves for same user (different amounts or token bundles)
User can submit proofs for each leaf during claim window

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: ERC20Airdrop2.claim and MerkleClaimable._verifyClaim leaf hash scheme
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: per-leaf replay protection with per-user additive balance
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: multi-leaf per user results in unintended cumulative claims
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no per-user 'hasClaimed' boolean; no constraint that (user) is unique

analysis_questions: Is the intended allocation model exactly one leaf per user?
How is the merkle tree constructed (offchain tooling) and does it enforce uniqueness?
Could setConfig updates introduce a second leaf for an already-eligible user?
validation_requirements: Review airdrop merkle generation scripts/specs for uniqueness guarantees
Construct a test root with two leaves for same user and show cumulative claimedAmount increase
Assess whether this violates distribution assumptions or caps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the published root accidentally contains two leaves for attacker address (user, amount1) and (user, amount2), attacker submits both proofs and doubles allocation; later withdraws during withdrawal window.
prerequisites: Merkle root includes multiple leaves per user (operational mistake or intentional design)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Material over-distribution of airdrop tokens, draining vault allowance reserved for other users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Potentially all users if vault runs out / allowance depleted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires merkle root correction and/or contract upgrade; already claimed amounts are irreversible

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing tests for duplicated user entries in the Merkle tree

threats.title: MerkleClaimable.setConfig() lacks sanity checks (start/end/root), enabling accidental or malicious permanent claim lockout
threats.hypothesis_description: MerkleClaimable._setConfig directly assigns claimStart/claimEnd/merkleRoot without validating ordering (claimStart < claimEnd), nonzero root, or future/valid ranges. A misconfiguration can make ongoingClaim always revert (e.g., claimStart > claimEnd, claimEnd=0, merkleRoot=0), halting claims/withdrawals that depend on claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a correctness defect in an admin-exposed setter that impacts core functionality. Even without a malicious owner, operational mistakes are common during parameter updates. Because claims are time-bound, a brief invalid config can permanently exclude users if claim window expires.
deployment_assumptions: Owner uses setConfig post-deployment to update root/times
Operational processes may accidentally set inconsistent timestamps or a zero root
threats.config_evidence: MerkleClaimable.sol _setConfig has no require checks; deployment script DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol initializes with zeros, implying a later setConfig is expected and thus configuration risk is realistic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _setConfig assigns values with no validation; ongoingClaim reverts if any are zero or time window invalid

preconditions: Owner (or governance) calls setConfig with invalid params (accident or compromise)
Users rely on claiming during a fixed window

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no input validation on critical configuration
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: claims can be globally bricked by invalid timestamps/root
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no require(claimStart < claimEnd), require(merkleRoot != 0), or event detailing config changes for monitoring

analysis_questions: Is there an operational process ensuring correct setConfig values before execution (e.g., timelock simulation)?
Should claimStart/claimEnd be immutable after start?
Do derived airdrop contracts need additional safeguards (e.g., only setConfig before claimStart)?
validation_requirements: Unit tests asserting setConfig rejects invalid ranges
Simulate misconfig and evaluate whether users can still claim/withdraw
Check if any emergency recovery mechanism exists (pause/unpause doesn't fix invalid window)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance executes a setConfig update with claimStart accidentally greater than claimEnd; all claims revert, and by the time corrected, claimEnd has passed for some users, causing permanent denial.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: users cannot claim allocated airdrop; tokens remain in vault but distribution fails
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Config can be fixed if owner still controls; missed windows may be irrecoverable without root/time reset

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing negative tests for invalid claim windows and zero roots

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: observable via config values + reverted claims

threats.title: TaikoL2._calcPublicInputHash ring-buffer indexing may compute inconsistent hashes for small _blockId values, risking chain halt if edge cases are reachable
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL2._calcPublicInputHash fills inputs[j % 255] for j = _blockId - i - 1. For small _blockId values near genesis, the loop bounds (_blockId >= i+1) and later assignment inputs[_blockId % 255] = blockhash(_blockId) can produce special-case behavior that must match the initialization path. Any mismatch between how publicInputHash is set in init() and recomputed in anchor() would cause L2_PUBLIC_INPUT_HASH_MISMATCH and permanently brick anchoring (chain halt).
threats.investigation_rationale: INV-STATE-003 indicates anchoring is irrecoverable on mismatch. The code handles block.number 0/1 specially in init, and anchor assumes an ongoing consistent ring-buffer. Edge-condition bugs here are catastrophic.
deployment_assumptions: The contract is initialized in a genesis-like environment where block.number may be 0 or 1, and subsequent anchor calls occur at expected heights
No L2 client differences exist in blockhash availability semantics during early blocks
threats.config_evidence: TaikoL2.init has special handling for block.number==0 and ==1; mismatched environment assumptions could break later anchor checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: genesis-specific init sets l2Hashes for parentHeight only in block.number==1; publicInputHash computed from _calcPublicInputHash(block.number); later anchor verifies against _calcPublicInputHash(parentId)

preconditions: A deployment or fork scenario where init() executes under unexpected block.number / blockhash semantics
anchor() is subsequently called and relies on publicInputHash matching

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: state machine depends on exact hash chain across genesis edge cases
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: any off-by-one or initialization mismatch bricks anchor forever
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no admin recovery/reset mechanism for publicInputHash without upgrading

analysis_questions: Does init() set publicInputHash in a way that exactly matches anchor() expectations at the first anchorable block?
What is the exact first block where anchor is called on Taiko L2, and what blockhashes are available there?
Are there L2-specific deviations where blockhash(0/1) differs or is unavailable?
validation_requirements: Simulate genesis + first N blocks and compute publicInputHash transitions
Property test: after each anchor, publicInputHash equals expectedOld for next call
Cross-client test if multiple Taiko clients exist

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If publicInputHash is initialized under a different assumption (e.g., block.number not 0/1), the first anchor reverts with L2_PUBLIC_INPUT_HASH_MISMATCH, halting anchoring and thereby halting cross-chain sync and basefee checks.
prerequisites: Misdeployment or environment mismatch (not attacker-controlled in normal conditions)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium-to-high systemic: anchoring halts; L2 operations relying on anchor-derived state roots may stall

threats.test_coverage_gap: Genesis-edge-case tests across real deployment conditions may be incomplete

threats.title: EssentialContract nonReentrant lock uses transient storage only on chainid==1, risking inconsistent behavior across L2s and potential reentrancy assumptions mismatch in inherited contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: EssentialContract._storeReentryLock/_loadReentryLock uses transient storage (tstore/tload) only when block.chainid == 1, otherwise it uses a storage slot (__reentry). On non-mainnet chains, the reentrancy guard persists in storage and is not transient per-transaction in the same way. If contract logic (or future upgrades) assumes transient semantics (e.g., cleared automatically at end of tx even across internal call patterns), subtle differences may allow unexpected reentrancy or lock-stuck scenarios if storage is corrupted via upgrade/storage collision.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a cross-chain behavioral divergence in a security primitive. While storage-based locks are common, mixing transient and persistent storage increases the chance of incorrect assumptions, especially across upgrades (storage collisions) or chain forks where chainid changes.
deployment_assumptions: Contracts are deployed on non-1 chain IDs (Taiko L2/L3) where storage-based lock is used
Future upgrades or inheritance changes could introduce storage layout issues affecting __reentry
Security review relies on transient-storage properties
threats.config_evidence: foundry.toml sets evm_version=cancun (transient storage available), but code explicitly limits transient usage to chainid==1, implying production on L2s uses storage lock.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (block.chainid == 1) use tstore/tload else use persistent storage __reentry

preconditions: Deployment on a chain with chainid != 1 (typical for Taiko L2/L3)
A reentrancy-sensitive function relies on this guard behaving as expected
Potential storage collision or unexpected write to __reentry (via upgrade bug) occurs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: security primitive differs by chainid; persistent storage lock more exposed to layout collisions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: upgrade/storage collision could freeze contract (lock stuck) or disable lock
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no explicit storage slot for __reentry beyond linear layout; relies on correct upgrade discipline

analysis_questions: Do any deployed environments have chainid==1 where transient is used, versus L2s where storage is used?
Are storage layouts for all UUPS upgrades verified to preserve __reentry slot?
Can any function write __reentry accidentally via storage collision or assembly?
validation_requirements: Storage layout diff checks across current and planned upgrades
Invariant tests: reentrancy lock is always cleared on all revert paths
Fuzz reentrancy attempts on key nonReentrant functions (TaikoL2.anchor/withdraw, airdrop claims)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an upgrade introduces a storage collision overwriting __reentry to _TRUE, all nonReentrant functions become permanently unusable (DoS). If overwritten to a value not equal to _TRUE, reentrancy guard may be bypassed in some paths.
prerequisites: Upgrade or storage layout mistake (not attacker-controlled directly)
Attacker exploits resulting weakened reentrancy protections if any exist

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: could brick withdrawals/anchoring/claims or expose reentrancy if guard is bypassed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of any contract inheriting EssentialContract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Upgrade could fix if upgrade path still functional

threats.test_coverage_gap: Storage layout regression tests for security-critical slots may be missing

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: reentrancy issues observable; lock-stuck observable via persistent reverts

threats.title: SigVerifyLib.verifyRS256Signature/verifyRS1Signature assumes 3-byte exponent, enabling signature verification failures or unexpected acceptance if exponent length differs
threats.hypothesis_description: SigVerifyLib slices the RSA public key as exponent=publicKey.substring(0,3) and modulus=remaining. This hard-codes exponent length to 3 bytes (commonly 0x010001), but X.509 RSA exponents can be encoded with different lengths. If a certificate chain includes a different exponent length, verification will either revert (substring bounds) or verify against an incorrectly parsed exponent/modulus pair, potentially causing false negatives (DoS) or, in worst case, mismatched verification behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: Hard-coded parsing assumptions in cryptographic verification are brittle. Even if Intel certs currently use 65537, any change or alternate CA could break verification. If verification is used beyond SGX certs, this is a general defect.
deployment_assumptions: The RSA publicKey format passed into SigVerifyLib is not strictly fixed to 3-byte exponent
Protocol uses SigVerifyLib for RSA certificate verification in contexts where exponent length can vary
threats.config_evidence: No explicit enforcement in ISigVerifyLib.PublicKey that exponent is exactly 3 bytes; parsing is internal to SigVerifyLib.

threats.title: PEMCertChainLib splitCertificateChain can desync parsing boundaries, enabling malformed PEM chains that pass split but decode into unexpected DER segments

threats.title: Triangular Double-Counting of L1 Block Issuance Drains EIP-1559 Gas Excess
threats.hypothesis_description: The EIP-1559 base fee calculation incorrectly accumulates L1 block issuance due to delayed updates of `lastSyncedBlock`. threats.title: Precision Loss in Vesting Cost Calculation Allows Fractional Token Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: In `TimelockTokenPool`, the cost required to withdraw tokens is calculated as `costToWithdraw = (amountUnlocked / 1e18) * costPerToken - costPaid`. Because the division by `1e18` occurs before multiplication, the fractional part of `amountUnlocked` (up to `0.999... * 1e18` wei) is truncated. A user can withdraw their entire `amountUnlocked` (including the fractional part) but will only pay for the integer part. At the end of the vesting period, the user permanently avoids paying `costPerToken` for the fractional remainder of their total grant.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct economic exploit allowing users to extract value from the protocol without paying the required cost. If `costPerToken` is high (e.g., priced in USDC), the stolen value per user is significant.

preconditions: Grant amount has a fractional component (not perfectly divisible by 1e18)
costPerToken > 0

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User extracts up to (1e18 - 1) wei of tokens for free, saving up to ~1 * costPerToken

threats.title: Gas Griefing via ES256Verifier Panic Halts Proving
threats.hypothesis_description: The `verifyES256Signature` function uses `assert(success)` after a `staticcall` to the external `ES256VERIFIER` (P256 precompile). If the precompile fails (e.g., due to an invalid public key not on the curve), `success` is false. The `assert(false)` triggers a Panic (opcode 0xFE), which consumes 100% of the transaction's allocated gas. An attacker can submit a malformed SGX quote that causes this panic, forcing the prover who attempts to verify the block to lose their entire gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Provers must submit proofs on L1, which costs significant gas. If an attacker can inject a transaction into the L2 block that causes the L1 proof verification to panic, they can economically drain the provers, halting the network.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool success, bytes memory ret) = ES256VERIFIER.staticcall(args); assert(success);

preconditions: Attacker can submit an L2 transaction containing a malformed SGX quote
Prover attempts to verify the block on L1

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: assert(success) instead of require(success)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Invalid P256 public key causes precompile to revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker spends minimal L2 gas to cost the prover maximum L1 gas (e.g., 30M gas)

economic_questions: Does the L2 node simulate and reject malformed quotes before inclusion?
What is the maximum gas limit a prover uses for L1 verification?
validation_requirements: Test P256 precompile behavior with invalid curve points
Verify that assert(false) consumes all gas in the current EVM version

threats.title: Strict Sequential txId Enforcement Causes Cross-Chain Head-of-Line Blocking
threats.hypothesis_description: In `CrossChainOwned`, `onMessageInvocation` enforces strict sequential execution via `if (txId != nextTxId) revert`. If the inner `address(this).call(txdata)` reverts, the entire transaction reverts, and `nextTxId` is not incremented. The bridge message remains FAILED/RETRIABLE. If the transaction cannot be made to succeed (e.g., it attempts to set a config that is no longer valid due to external state changes), `nextTxId` is permanently stuck. All subsequent cross-chain admin commands will fail because their `txId` will be greater than the stuck `nextTxId`.
threats.investigation_rationale: Admin channels must be robust. Bricking the cross-chain ownership channel prevents the DAO/Admin from upgrading contracts, pausing the system during an exploit, or rescuing funds.

threats.title: Extending claimEnd Causes Underflow and Locks Airdrop Withdrawals
threats.hypothesis_description: In `ERC20Airdrop2`, the `timeBasedAllowance` is calculated relative to `claimEnd`. If the admin uses `setConfig` to extend `claimEnd` to a later date, the calculated `timeBasedAllowance` for all users will instantly decrease. threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation._checkTcbLevels Returns True for Unrecognized TCB Status
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkTcbLevels function returns (true, TCBStatus.TCB_UNRECOGNIZED) when no TCB level matches the PCK certificate's SVN values. threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete logic error in attestation verification code. The boolean return value contradicts the semantic meaning of TCB_UNRECOGNIZED. threats.title: QE Report TCB Level Selection Depends on Array Ordering - Wrong Status May Be Selected
threats.hypothesis_description: In _verifyQEReportWithIdentity, the function iterates through enclaveId.tcbLevels and selects the FIRST entry where tcb.tcb.isvsvn <= quoteEnclaveReport.isvSvn. For example, if a revoked level appears before a valid level with a lower SVN threshold, the revoked status would be returned even for legitimate quotes.

threats.title: verifyParsedQuote Dual Entry Path Bypasses Raw Quote Binary Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation exposes two entry points: verifyAttestation (raw bytes → parseInput → _verifyParsedQuote) and verifyParsedQuote (pre-parsed struct → validateParsedInput → _verifyParsedQuote). The verifyParsedQuote path accepts a pre-parsed struct and reconstructs signedQuoteData via abi.encodePacked, which may produce different bytes than the original SGX binary format. Specifically, parseInput validates localAuthDataSize consistency, binary length checks, and header parsing from raw bytes, none of which are enforced in the pre-parsed path. The reconstructed signedQuoteData is used for signature verification, creating a potential semantic gap between what was signed and what is verified.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the reconstructed bytes differ from the actual signed quote binary, signature verification could either incorrectly pass (if an attacker controls the attestation key) or incorrectly fail (DoS). The dual-path design introduces a subtle verification asymmetry that could be exploited in the SGX proving pipeline.

threats.title: V3Parser.parseCerificationChainBytes Makes External Calls in Pure Context
threats.hypothesis_description: V3Parser.parseCerificationChainBytes is declared as an internal pure function but instantiates PEMCertChainLib(pemCertLibAddr) and calls splitCertificateChain() on it — an external contract call. While the called function is also declared pure, the actual execution at the EVM level involves a STATICCALL to an external address. The pemCertLibAddr is passed as a parameter through parseInput, meaning the address of the certificate parsing library is determined at call time. If a malicious address is provided (e.g., through a crafted verifyParsedQuote call), the external call could return manipulated certificate data that passes subsequent verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pure function modifier creates a false sense of determinism. The function's behavior depends on the code at pemCertLibAddr, which is an input parameter. While verifyAttestation uses the immutable pemCertLib address, verifyParsedQuote receives decodedCertDataArray already parsed, bypassing this code path. Understanding this interaction is critical for assessing the attestation system's integrity.

preconditions: parseInput is called with a pemCertLibAddr pointing to a contract with manipulated splitCertificateChain
OR the immutable pemCertLib address is correct but the pure modifier creates optimization risks

threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Has No Ownership Transfer Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation uses a custom owner pattern set in the constructor with no transfer or renounce mechanism. Unlike EssentialContract-based contracts which inherit Ownable2Step, this contract's owner is permanently fixed. threats.title: Unbounded Gas Consumption in ES256 Signature Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: The `verifyES256Signature` function uses `assert(success)` after a `staticcall` to the external `ES256VERIFIER` contract. If the verifier contract is missing, destroyed, or reverts, the `staticcall` returns `false`, and `assert(success)` compiles to the `INVALID` opcode, which consumes all remaining transaction gas. This creates a griefing vector where failed attestations cost users the maximum possible gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using `assert` instead of `require` for external calls violates best practices and creates unnecessary financial penalties for users submitting invalid or unsupported attestations.
deployment_assumptions: ES256VERIFIER contract is not deployed, self-destructs, or reverts on specific inputs
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys P256Verifier, but if it fails or is misconfigured, the assert will trigger.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assert(success) consumes all gas on staticcall failure

preconditions: ES256VERIFIER address has no code or reverts during execution
User submits an attestation requiring ES256 verification

threats.title: EssentialContract pause/unpause are public entry points; derived contracts may unintentionally expose pausing where owner is cross-chain, creating liveness hazards

threats.title: TimelockTokenPool.void() Interaction Allows Owner to Manipulate Vesting Post-Void
threats.hypothesis_description: When void() is called, _voidGrant sets _grant.amount = amountOwned (what's already vested), then zeros grantStart and grantPeriod. After voiding, _getAmountOwned returns _grant.amount directly (since _start == 0 → return _amount). However, the unlock schedule (unlockStart/unlockCliff/unlockPeriod) remains unchanged. The voided recipient's tokens become fully 'owned' but still subject to the original unlock schedule. If the owner calls void() strategically (right before a vesting cliff), the recipient loses tokens they were about to vest. The owner could re-grant these voided tokens to a colluding party.
threats.investigation_rationale: The void mechanism allows the owner to claw back unvested tokens at any point. Combined with the ability to re-grant voided tokens (totalAmountVoided increases, freeing capacity for new grants), this creates an asymmetric power dynamic where the owner can strategically time voids to maximize clawback.

threats.hypothesis_description: The toggleLocalReportCheck() function flips _checkLocalEnclaveReport between true and false. The toggle mechanism allows this check to be completely disabled. Since the default is off (_checkLocalEnclaveReport = false), the enclave identity verification may never be active unless explicitly enabled.

threats.title: MerkleClaimable.setConfig() Can Invalidate Active Claims by Changing Merkle Root
threats.hypothesis_description: The owner can call setConfig() at any time to change merkleRoot, claimStart, and claimEnd without constraints. Changing the merkle root mid-claim period invalidates all unclaimed entries from the previous tree. Users who had valid proofs become unable to claim. The owner could also set claimStart > claimEnd or merkleRoot to bytes32(0), which triggers the ongoingClaim modifier to revert for all claims. Already-claimed entries remain marked (isClaimed[hash] = true), but these hashes are tied to the old root's leaf structure.
threats.investigation_rationale: For ERC20Airdrop, ERC20Airdrop2, and ERC721Airdrop contracts, the merkle root change can permanently lock out eligible users from their airdrop allocations. Combined with the deployment evidence showing init with merkleRoot = bytes32(0), there's a temporal gap where claims are impossible until owner sets a valid config.

threats.title: Irrevocable Attestation Configuration due to Missing Ownership Transfer Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: The `AutomataDcapV3Attestation` contract sets the owner in the constructor but provides no mechanism to transfer or renounce ownership. If the owner key is compromised, the attacker gains permanent control.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike other protocol contracts that use `Ownable2StepUpgradeable`, this contract uses a rigid ownership model. Key compromise leads to an unrecoverable state where the attacker can manipulate trusted enclaves and certificate revocations indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = msg.sender;

preconditions: The owner's private key is compromised or lost.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing `transferOwnership` function in a contract with critical administrative privileges.

Is the owner a multisig or an EOA?

threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation trust-liveness failure due to non-transferable owner (no rotation) for TCB/QE identity updates
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation uses a custom owner variable set only in the constructor and provides no ownership transfer/rotation function. If the owner key is lost, the system cannot update TCB info, QE identity, revocation lists, or enclave allowlists. Over time (as Intel rotates collateral, revokes certs, or updates TCB levels), attestations may begin to fail permanently or remain insecure (unable to revoke).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operational code defect: governance/maintenance cannot be performed after a single key loss. It creates an eventual availability/security cliff for SGX verification.
deployment_assumptions: AutomataDcapV3Attestation is used in production as a required attestation authority
Owner is a single EOA or otherwise not operationally resilient
Collateral updates/revocations are expected over the contract lifetime
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys AutomataDcapV3Attestation with owner = deployer EOA (msg.sender) and no subsequent ownership transfer is shown.

threats.title: Airdrop claim window can be permanently disabled or made inconsistent due to missing sanity checks in MerkleClaimable.setConfig
threats.hypothesis_description: MerkleClaimable.setConfig and _setConfig do not validate that claimStart < claimEnd, or that merkleRoot is non-zero when enabling claims. Misconfiguration (or malicious owner action) can set invalid parameters (e.g., claimStart > claimEnd or merkleRoot=0) causing ongoingClaim to revert for all claim attempts, effectively locking distribution until reconfigured.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operational defect that can halt airdrop distribution and generate user-facing incidents. It is especially relevant because DeployERC20Airdrop initializes with zeros for claimStart/claimEnd/root, relying on a later setConfig call.
deployment_assumptions: Owner might accidentally set invalid claimStart/claimEnd or clear merkleRoot during an active distribution
No external safety checks (timelock/process) prevent such changes
threats.config_evidence: DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol initializes ERC20Airdrop with (claimStart=0, claimEnd=0, merkleRoot=0) implying later setConfig is required; MerkleClaimable._setConfig has no validation.

proof_of_signal.lines: _setConfig sets claimStart/claimEnd/merkleRoot directly; ongoingClaim reverts if any are 0 or time bounds invalid
proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(_claimStart < _claimEnd); no require(_merkleRoot != 0)

preconditions: Owner calls setConfig with invalid parameters (accidental or malicious)
Users rely on on-chain claim path without alternative distribution mechanism
No upgrade/emergency override process immediately available

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contract is deployed in a disabled state by script (0,0,0 root) and must be configured correctly later; a single bad setConfig halts all claims.

operational_questions: Is setConfig controlled by a timelock or multisig in production deployments?
Are there monitoring/alerts preventing claim window misconfiguration?
Is there an emergency migration plan if claim parameters are set incorrectly?
